summary: I was born in Japan, specifically in Osaka Prefecture, and grew up there before coming to America. My memories of Japan include my father's participation in the Russo-Japanese War and the cultural practice of Ohyakudo-mairi to pray for his safe return. Even after moving to America, I often thought of Japan as my homeland, especially Nara Prefecture where my husband was born.

After leaving Japan, I went directly to Cincinnati where my husband worked as a cook in the house of Mr. Julian, a statesman. By the time we returned, Julian had been elected Secretary of the Treasury. It was a Christian household, and important events were marked by inviting a pastor to pray with the family.

My husband worked as a cook for Mr. Julian, who was a statesman in Cincinnati. Later, Julian was elected Secretary of the Treasury. I remember the Christian atmosphere in his home, where a pastor was often invited to pray during important family occasions.

Mr. Julian, for whom my husband worked as a cook, was eventually elected Secretary of the Treasury. This was during the time we lived in Cincinnati. The role was significant as it reflected Julian's political stature and the respect he commanded in his Christian household.

I was born in Osaka Prefecture and have fond memories of the area. One vivid memory is when my grandmother took me to Osaka to see an airplane fly for the first time in Japan, although it crashed. Osaka felt like a nice place to me even then.

Though I was born in Osaka Prefecture, I consider myself from Nara Prefecture because my husband Tomita was born there and since coming to America, I have identified as a Nara-Ken jin. Whenever asked where I am from, I naturally say Nara Prefecture.

My husband was living in Yakima, Washington, which is a bit inland from Seattle, when our marriage was arranged. After we married in autumn 1920, we left for America in spring 1921 and lived in Yakima working as farmers for eight years before moving closer to Seattle.

After farming in Yakima for eight years, my husband and I moved to the suburbs of Seattle. This move marked a new chapter in our life in America. Seattle was more urban compared to Yakima, and we settled there with hopes for a better future.

I know of a man named Hidaka who is a friend of my father and lives in Kagoshima. There are quite a few people with the surname Hidaka in that area, even if they are not related. It was interesting to learn that many Hidakas had gone abroad from there.

When I was young, I visited Minatogawa in the Kobe area, which is famous for Masashige Kusunoki, a historically brave samurai. That place left a strong impression on me, and I admired Kusunoki's legacy as a symbol of courage and loyalty.

I have memories of Kobe, especially when I visited Minatogawa, a place known for Masashige Kusunoki. Also, my family sought medical treatment in Kobe when my father became blind. Kobe was an important city for both cultural and health reasons in my life.

Osaka is where I was born and where I have many memories. One remarkable event was seeing an airplane fly for the first time in Japan there, although it crashed. Osaka always felt like a lively and interesting place to me during my childhood.

My father fought in the Russo-Japanese War of 1904-1905, and before that, he also participated in the Sino-Japanese War. During the Russo-Japanese War, our family practiced Ohyakudo-mairi, praying at the shrine a hundred times for his safe return. It was a difficult time, especially as a child, to understand the sadness and honor tied to the war.

Before I was born in 1896, my father had already fought in the Sino-Japanese War. This earlier war was part of my family's history and shaped my father's experiences. It was a source of pride but also sorrow for our family.

During the Russo-Japanese War, it was considered an honor to fight for the Emperor of Japan, and people were expected not to cry in public when their family members went to war. This cultural expectation shaped how my family and community behaved, even though we cried secretly at home.

Ohyakudo-mairi is a practice where one walks around a shrine a hundred times praying for a specific wish. When my father went to war, I was only seven and used to do Ohyakudo-mairi to pray for his safe return. This ritual made me realize how serious and sad war really was, even as a child.

I experienced World War II about 30 years ago, and the bitterness of war deeply affected me. Having seen wars before, including my father's participation in earlier conflicts, I strongly believe that war should never happen again. The suffering and sadness left a lasting impression on my mind.

After marrying my husband Tomita in Japan, we came to America in the spring of 1921. We first lived in Yakima, Washington, working as farmers for eight years before moving to Seattle. America became our new home where we built our life together as immigrants.

When I was in sixth grade, a teacher took us to downtown Hiroshima to see new buildings and illuminations. We visited the Chinretsukan Museum, which later became the only building left standing after the atomic bomb was dropped. Visiting that place years later reminded me of my childhood and the changes Hiroshima endured.

As a child, I visited the Chinretsukan Museum in Hiroshima with my school classes. The museum was made of reinforced concrete and survived the atomic bombing, with its iron dome frame still intact. Visiting it again years later brought back memories of my youth and the city's tragic history.

When my father became blind from cataracts, he tried everything to recover his sight, including going on a pilgrimage to Shikoku. This pilgrimage was part of his efforts to find healing, showing how important faith and traditional practices were to our family.

My family sought medical treatment in Kobe when my father lost his sight due to cataracts. The doctor there was strict but dedicated, treating my burns and my father's condition. Kobe was a significant place for us in terms of health and healing.

I was born in the San-in district, which is known for its cold winters and snow. One winter night before I started school, I suffered burns from a charcoal brazier accident and my mother took me to a doctor despite the harsh weather. The cold and rural conditions of San-in shaped many of my early experiences.
